sentences.zh: 殷契，母曰簡狄，有娀氏之女，為帝嚳次妃。

translations.literal: Yin Xie: his mother was called Jiandi, a daughter of the Yousong clan, and she was Di Ku's second consort.
translations.idiomatic: Xie of Yin had a mother named Jiandi, of the Yousong clan, who was Di Ku's junior consort.

sentences.zh: 三人行浴，見玄鳥墮其卵，簡狄取吞之，因孕生契。

translations.literal: The three of them went to bathe; seeing the dark bird drop its egg, Jiandi took and swallowed it; thereby pregnant she bore Xie.
translations.idiomatic: While Jiandi bathed with two companions, a dark bird dropped an egg; she took it, swallowed it, and conceived Xie.

sentences.zh: 契長而佐禹治水有功。

translations.literal: Xie grew up and assisted Yu in regulating the waters with merit.
translations.idiomatic: When Xie came of age he helped Yu manage the flood and earned distinction.

sentences.zh: 帝舜乃命契曰：「百姓不親，五品不訓，汝為司徒而敬敷五教，五教在寬。」

translations.literal: Emperor Shun thereupon charged Xie, saying, "The hundred surnames are not kind; the five grades lack instruction—you shall be Minister of Education and reverently spread the five teachings; the five teachings lie in breadth."
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Shun told Xie: The people lack harmony and the five relationships lack guidance; as Minister of Education spread the five moral lessons with generous patience.

sentences.zh: 封于商，賜姓子氏。

translations.literal: He was enfeoffed at Shang and granted the surname Zi.
translations.idiomatic: He received the fief of Shang and the clan name Zi.

sentences.zh: 契興於唐、虞、大禹之際，功業著於百姓，百姓以平。

translations.literal: Xie rose in the interval of Tang, Yu, and the Great Yu; his achievements shone among the hundred surnames; by them the people were pacified.
translations.idiomatic: He flourished from the reigns of Yao and Shun through Great Yu; his deeds won renown among the folk and kept the realm at peace.

translations.literal: Yin Xie: his mother was called Jiandi, a daughter of the Yousong clan, and she was Di Ku's second consort. The three of them went to bathe; seeing the dark bird drop its egg, Jiandi took and swallowed it; thereby pregnant she bore Xie. Xie grew up and assisted Yu in regulating the waters with merit. Emperor Shun thereupon charged Xie, saying, "The hundred surnames are not kind; the five grades lack instruction—you shall be Minister of Education and reverently spread the five teachings; the five teachings lie in breadth." He was enfeoffed at Shang and granted the surname Zi. Xie rose in the interval of Tang, Yu, and the Great Yu; his achievements shone among the hundred surnames; by them the people were pacified.
translations.idiomatic: Xie of Yin had a mother named Jiandi, of the Yousong clan, who was Di Ku's junior consort. While Jiandi bathed with two companions, a dark bird dropped an egg; she took it, swallowed it, and conceived Xie. When Xie came of age he helped Yu manage the flood and earned distinction. Emperor Shun told Xie: The people lack harmony and the five relationships lack guidance; as Minister of Education spread the five moral lessons with generous patience. He received the fief of Shang and the clan name Zi. He flourished from the reigns of Yao and Shun through Great Yu; his deeds won renown among the folk and kept the realm at peace.

sentences.zh: 契卒，子昭明立。

translations.literal: Xie passed away; his son Zhaoming established himself.
translations.idiomatic: When Xie died, his son Zhaoming succeeded him.

sentences.zh: 昭明卒，子相土立。

translations.literal: Zhaoming passed away; his son Xiang Tu established himself.
translations.idiomatic: Zhaoming was succeeded by his son Xiang Tu.

sentences.zh: 相土卒，子昌若立。

translations.literal: Xiang Tu passed away; his son Changruo established himself.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Tu was succeeded by his son Changruo.

sentences.zh: 昌若卒，子曹圉立。

translations.literal: Changruo passed away; his son Caoyu established himself.
translations.idiomatic: Changruo was succeeded by his son Caoyu.

sentences.zh: 曹圉卒，子冥立。

translations.literal: Caoyu passed away; his son Ming established himself.
translations.idiomatic: Caoyu was succeeded by his son Ming.

sentences.zh: 冥卒，子振立。

translations.literal: Ming passed away; his son Zhen established himself.
translations.idiomatic: Ming was succeeded by his son Zhen.

sentences.zh: 振卒，子微立。

translations.literal: Zhen passed away; his son Wei established himself.
translations.idiomatic: Zhen was succeeded by his son Wei.

sentences.zh: 微卒，子報丁立。

translations.literal: Wei passed away; his son Baoding established himself.
translations.idiomatic: Wei was succeeded by his son Baoding.

sentences.zh: 報丁卒，子報乙立。

translations.literal: Baoding passed away; his son Baoyi established himself.
translations.idiomatic: Baoding was succeeded by his son Baoyi.

sentences.zh: 報乙卒，子報丙立。

translations.literal: Baoyi passed away; his son Baobing established himself.
translations.idiomatic: Baoyi was succeeded by his son Baobing.

sentences.zh: 報丙卒，子主壬立。

translations.literal: Baobing passed away; his son Zhuren established himself.
translations.idiomatic: Baobing was succeeded by his son Zhuren.

sentences.zh: 主壬卒，子主癸立。

translations.literal: Zhuren passed away; his son Zhugui established himself.
translations.idiomatic: Zhuren was succeeded by his son Zhugui.

sentences.zh: 主癸卒，子天乙立，是為成湯。

translations.literal: Zhugui passed away; his son Tianyi established himself; this is Cheng Tang.
translations.idiomatic: Zhugui was succeeded by his son Tianyi—who is known as Cheng Tang.

translations.literal: Xie passed away; his son Zhaoming established himself. Zhaoming passed away; his son Xiang Tu established himself. Xiang Tu passed away; his son Changruo established himself. Changruo passed away; his son Caoyu established himself. Caoyu passed away; his son Ming established himself. Ming passed away; his son Zhen established himself. Zhen passed away; his son Wei established himself. Wei passed away; his son Baoding established himself. Baoding passed away; his son Baoyi established himself. Baoyi passed away; his son Baobing established himself. Baobing passed away; his son Zhuren established himself. Zhuren passed away; his son Zhugui established himself. Zhugui passed away; his son Tianyi established himself; this is Cheng Tang.
translations.idiomatic: When Xie died, his son Zhaoming succeeded him. Zhaoming was succeeded by his son Xiang Tu. Xiang Tu was succeeded by his son Changruo. Changruo was succeeded by his son Caoyu. Caoyu was succeeded by his son Ming. Ming was succeeded by his son Zhen. Zhen was succeeded by his son Wei. Wei was succeeded by his son Baoding. Baoding was succeeded by his son Baoyi. Baoyi was succeeded by his son Baobing. Baobing was succeeded by his son Zhuren. Zhuren was succeeded by his son Zhugui. Zhugui was succeeded by his son Tianyi—who is known as Cheng Tang.

sentences.zh: 成湯，自契至湯八遷。

translations.literal: Cheng Tang—from Xie to Tang eight migrations.
translations.idiomatic: From Xie to Tang the seat of the line had shifted eight times.

sentences.zh: 湯始居亳，從先王居，作帝誥。

translations.literal: Tang first dwelt at Bo, following former kings in residence; he made "Di's Announcement."
translations.idiomatic: Tang first settled at Bo beside the graves of his ancestors and composed the Announcement to Di.

translations.literal: Cheng Tang—from Xie to Tang eight migrations. Tang first dwelt at Bo, following former kings in residence; he made "Di's Announcement."
translations.idiomatic: From Xie to Tang the seat of the line had shifted eight times. Tang first settled at Bo beside the graves of his ancestors and composed the Announcement to Di.

sentences.zh: 湯征諸侯。

translations.literal: Tang campaigned against the lords.
translations.idiomatic: Tang led punitive expeditions against the regional lords.

sentences.zh: 葛伯不祀，湯始伐之。

translations.literal: Lord of Ge did not sacrifice; Tang first attacked him.
translations.idiomatic: When the lord of Ge failed to perform the sacrifices, Tang attacked him first.

sentences.zh: 湯曰：「予有言：人視水見形，視民知治不。」

translations.literal: Tang said, "I have a saying: people look on water and see form; look on the people and know order or not."
translations.idiomatic: Tang said: Men look into water to see their faces; look to the people to know whether rule is sound.

sentences.zh: 伊尹曰：「明哉！

translations.literal: Yi Yin said, "How clear!
translations.idiomatic: Yi Yin replied: How clear—

sentences.zh: 言能聽，道乃進。

translations.literal: When words can be heard, the way then advances.
translations.idiomatic: when counsel is heard, the Way moves forward.

sentences.zh: 君國子民，為善者皆在王官。

translations.literal: Ruler of the state, father to the people—all who do good are at the royal court.
translations.idiomatic: The lord who fathers his people gathers every worthy into his court.

sentences.zh: 勉哉，勉哉！」

translations.literal: Exert yourself, exert yourself!"
translations.idiomatic: Strive, my lord—never slacken.

sentences.zh: 湯曰：「汝不能敬命，予大罰殛之，無有攸赦。」

translations.literal: Tang said, "You who fail to reverence the command—I will greatly punish and kill you, without any sparing."
translations.idiomatic: Tang warned: Whoever spurns my command I will strike down without mercy.

sentences.zh: 作湯征。

translations.literal: He made "Tang's Punitive Expeditions."
translations.idiomatic: He composed the Tang Punitive Expeditions.

translations.literal: Tang campaigned against the lords. Lord of Ge did not sacrifice; Tang first attacked him. Tang said, "I have a saying: people look on water and see form; look on the people and know order or not." Yi Yin said, "How clear! When words can be heard, the way then advances. Ruler of the state, father to the people—all who do good are at the royal court. Exert yourself, exert yourself!" Tang said, "You who fail to reverence the command—I will greatly punish and kill you, without any sparing." He made "Tang's Punitive Expeditions."
translations.idiomatic: Tang led punitive expeditions against the regional lords. When the lord of Ge failed to perform the sacrifices, Tang attacked him first. Tang said: Men look into water to see their faces; look to the people to know whether rule is sound. Yi Yin replied: How clear— when counsel is heard, the Way moves forward. The lord who fathers his people gathers every worthy into his court. Strive, my lord—never slacken. Tang warned: Whoever spurns my command I will strike down without mercy. He composed the Tang Punitive Expeditions.

sentences.zh: 伊尹名阿衡。

translations.literal: Yi Yin's name was Aheng.
translations.idiomatic: Yi Yin was styled Aheng.

sentences.zh: 阿衡欲奸湯而無由，乃為有莘氏媵臣，負鼎俎，以滋味說湯，致于王道。

translations.literal: Aheng desired to attach himself to Tang but had no avenue; thereupon he became a bridesmen minister for the Youxin clan, carrying cauldron and stand, using flavors to persuade Tang, bringing him to the kingly way.
translations.idiomatic: Wishing to serve Tang but lacking an introduction, Aheng joined the Youxin bridal train as a cook, carried kettle and board, won Tang through his cooking, and drew him toward the kingly way.

sentences.zh: 或曰，伊尹處士，湯使人聘迎之，五反然後肯往從湯，言素王及九主之事。

translations.literal: Another says Yi Yin was a scholar-at-home; Tang sent men to invite him with gifts; five returns then he was willing to go and follow Tang, discoursing on the Uncrowned King and the nine lords.
translations.idiomatic: Others say Yi Yin was a recluse whom Tang invited five times before he came, then lectured on the model rulers and the Nine Masters.

sentences.zh: 湯舉任以國政。

translations.literal: Tang raised and entrusted him with state government.
translations.idiomatic: Tang handed him the administration of the realm.

sentences.zh: 伊尹去湯適夏。

translations.literal: Yi Yin left Tang for Xia.
translations.idiomatic: Yi Yin left Tang and went over to the Xia court.

sentences.zh: 既醜有夏，復歸于亳。

translations.literal: Having found fault with You-Xia, he returned to Bo.
translations.idiomatic: Once he had taken Xia's measure, he returned to Bo.

sentences.zh: 入自北門，遇女鳩、女房，作女鳩女房。

translations.literal: Entering by the north gate, he met Nüjiu and Nüfang; he made "Nüjiu and Nüfang."
translations.idiomatic: He entered by the north gate, met the ladies Jiuji and Fang, and composed the pieces Nüjiu and Nüfang.

translations.literal: Yi Yin's name was Aheng. Aheng desired to attach himself to Tang but had no avenue; thereupon he became a bridesmen minister for the Youxin clan, carrying cauldron and stand, using flavors to persuade Tang, bringing him to the kingly way. Another says Yi Yin was a scholar-at-home; Tang sent men to invite him with gifts; five returns then he was willing to go and follow Tang, discoursing on the Uncrowned King and the nine lords. Tang raised and entrusted him with state government. Yi Yin left Tang for Xia. Having found fault with You-Xia, he returned to Bo. Entering by the north gate, he met Nüjiu and Nüfang; he made "Nüjiu and Nüfang."
translations.idiomatic: Yi Yin was styled Aheng. Wishing to serve Tang but lacking an introduction, Aheng joined the Youxin bridal train as a cook, carried kettle and board, won Tang through his cooking, and drew him toward the kingly way. Others say Yi Yin was a recluse whom Tang invited five times before he came, then lectured on the model rulers and the Nine Masters. Tang handed him the administration of the realm. Yi Yin left Tang and went over to the Xia court. Once he had taken Xia's measure, he returned to Bo. He entered by the north gate, met the ladies Jiuji and Fang, and composed the pieces Nüjiu and Nüfang.

sentences.zh: 湯出，見野張網四面，祝曰：「自天下四方皆入吾網。」

translations.literal: Tang went out and saw the wilds spread with nets on four sides, praying saying, "From heaven below and the four quarters all enter my net."
translations.idiomatic: Tang found a hunter who had set nets on every side and prayed that all under heaven would fall into his snares.

sentences.zh: 湯曰：「嘻，盡之矣！」

translations.literal: Tang said, "Alas—that is utterly too much!"
translations.idiomatic: Tang cried: No—that would trap every living thing!

sentences.zh: 乃去其三面，祝曰：「欲左，左。

translations.literal: Thereupon he removed three sides, praying saying, "Want left, left."
translations.idiomatic: He had three sides taken down and called out: If you want left, go left.

sentences.zh: 欲右，右。

translations.literal: Want right, right.
translations.idiomatic: If you want right, go right.

sentences.zh: 不用命，乃入吾網。」

translations.literal: Not obeying command—then enter my net."
translations.idiomatic: Only those who defy my order need enter the net.

sentences.zh: 諸侯聞之，曰：「湯德至矣，及禽獸。」

translations.literal: The lords hearing it said, "Tang's virtue reaches even to birds and beasts."
translations.idiomatic: The lords said: Tang's kindness reaches even the birds and beasts.

translations.literal: Tang went out and saw the wilds spread with nets on four sides, praying saying, "From heaven below and the four quarters all enter my net." Tang said, "Alas—that is utterly too much!" Thereupon he removed three sides, praying saying, "Want left, left." Want right, right. Not obeying command—then enter my net." The lords hearing it said, "Tang's virtue reaches even to birds and beasts."
translations.idiomatic: Tang found a hunter who had set nets on every side and prayed that all under heaven would fall into his snares. Tang cried: No—that would trap every living thing! He had three sides taken down and called out: If you want left, go left. If you want right, go right. Only those who defy my order need enter the net. The lords said: Tang's kindness reaches even the birds and beasts.

sentences.zh: 當是時，夏桀為虐政淫荒，而諸侯昆吾氏為亂。

translations.literal: At this time Xia Jie practiced cruel government, dissolute and wild, and among the lords the Kunwu clan made rebellion.
translations.idiomatic: In those days Jie of Xia ruled with cruelty and excess while the Kunwu lords stirred rebellion.

sentences.zh: 湯乃興師率諸侯，伊尹從湯，湯自把鉞以伐昆吾，遂伐桀。

translations.literal: Tang thereupon raised troops leading the lords; Yi Yin followed Tang; Tang himself grasped the battle-axe to attack Kunwu, then attacked Jie.
translations.idiomatic: Tang then raised an army of the lords, Yi Yin at his side, and himself wielded the axe against Kunwu before marching on Jie.

sentences.zh: 湯曰：「格女眾庶，來，女悉聽朕言。

translations.literal: Tang said, "Come you multitude, come, you all listen to my words.
translations.idiomatic: Tang addressed the host: Come, all of you, and hear my words.

sentences.zh: 匪台小子敢行舉亂，有夏多罪，予維聞女眾言，夏氏有罪。

translations.literal: It is not I, the little child, who dare raise disorder; You-Xia has many crimes—I only have heard you multitudes speak—the Xia clan has guilt.
translations.idiomatic: It is not I who dare stir revolt; Xia is heavy with crime—I have heard you yourselves say the Xia house is guilty.

sentences.zh: 予畏上帝，不敢不正。

translations.literal: I fear Supreme God; I dare not fail to correct.
translations.idiomatic: I fear Heaven and dare not withhold punishment.

sentences.zh: 今夏多罪，天命殛之。

translations.literal: Now Xia has many crimes; Heaven's mandate is to strike it dead.
translations.idiomatic: Xia's crimes are many; Heaven has ordered its destruction.

sentences.zh: 今女有眾，女曰：『我君不恤我眾，捨我嗇事而割政』。

translations.literal: Now you have a multitude; you say, 'Our lord does not pity us multitudes, casts aside our harvest toil and cuts policy.'
translations.idiomatic: You say among yourselves: Our king does not pity us—he sets aside our field work and meddles with government.

sentences.zh: 女其曰：『有罪，其柰何』？

translations.literal: You will say, 'If there is guilt, what then shall be done?'
translations.idiomatic: You ask: If there is fault, what can be done?

sentences.zh: 夏王率止眾力，率奪夏國。

translations.literal: The Xia king has led astray and exhausted the multitude's strength, led and seized the Xia state.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Xia has drained your strength and plundered the Xia domain.

sentences.zh: 眾有率怠不和，曰：『是日何時喪？

translations.literal: The multitude has led slackness and discord, saying, 'When will this sun perish?'
translations.idiomatic: The people have grown slack and bitter, crying: When will this sun set?

sentences.zh: 予與女皆亡』！

translations.literal: I with you will all perish!'
translations.idiomatic: We would rather die with you than live under him!

sentences.zh: 夏德若茲，今朕必往。

translations.literal: Xia's virtue being thus, now I must go.
translations.idiomatic: Xia's conduct is such that I must march against it.

sentences.zh: 爾尚及予一人致天之罰，予其大理女。

translations.literal: You still may join me, the One Man, to inflict Heaven's punishment—I will greatly order you.
translations.idiomatic: Join me, the One Man, in carrying out Heaven's sentence, and I will reward you richly.

sentences.zh: 女毋不信，朕不食言。

translations.literal: You must not disbelieve—I do not break my word.
translations.idiomatic: Do not doubt me—I keep my oath.

sentences.zh: 女不從誓言，予則帑僇女，無有攸赦。」

translations.literal: You who do not follow the sworn words—I will put you and your kin to death, without any sparing."
translations.idiomatic: Whoever breaks this oath I will execute, you and your families, without mercy.

sentences.zh: 以告令師，作湯誓。

translations.literal: Thereby announcing to command the army; he made "Tang's Oath."
translations.idiomatic: He proclaimed this to the host and composed the Oath of Tang.

sentences.zh: 於是湯曰：「吾甚武」，號曰武王。

translations.literal: Thereupon Tang said, "I am very martial," and styled himself Martial King.
translations.idiomatic: Tang declared himself strongly martial and took the title Martial King.

translations.literal: At this time Xia Jie practiced cruel government, dissolute and wild, and among the lords the Kunwu clan made rebellion. Tang thereupon raised troops leading the lords; Yi Yin followed Tang; Tang himself grasped the battle-axe to attack Kunwu, then attacked Jie. Tang said, "Come you multitude, come, you all listen to my words. It is not I, the little child, who dare raise disorder; You-Xia has many crimes—I only have heard you multitudes speak—the Xia clan has guilt. I fear Supreme God; I dare not fail to correct. Now Xia has many crimes; Heaven's mandate is to strike it dead. Now you have a multitude; you say, 'Our lord does not pity us multitudes, casts aside our harvest toil and cuts policy.' You will say, 'If there is guilt, what then shall be done?' The Xia king has led astray and exhausted the multitude's strength, led and seized the Xia state. The multitude has led slackness and discord, saying, 'When will this sun perish?' I with you will all perish!' Xia's virtue being thus, now I must go. You still may join me, the One Man, to inflict Heaven's punishment—I will greatly order you. You must not disbelieve—I do not break my word. You who do not follow the sworn words—I will put you and your kin to death, without any sparing." Thereby announcing to command the army; he made "Tang's Oath." Thereupon Tang said, "I am very martial," and styled himself Martial King.
translations.idiomatic: In those days Jie of Xia ruled with cruelty and excess while the Kunwu lords stirred rebellion. Tang then raised an army of the lords, Yi Yin at his side, and himself wielded the axe against Kunwu before marching on Jie. Tang addressed the host: Come, all of you, and hear my words. It is not I who dare stir revolt; Xia is heavy with crime—I have heard you yourselves say the Xia house is guilty. I fear Heaven and dare not withhold punishment. Xia's crimes are many; Heaven has ordered its destruction. You say among yourselves: Our king does not pity us—he sets aside our field work and meddles with government. You ask: If there is fault, what can be done? The king of Xia has drained your strength and plundered the Xia domain. The people have grown slack and bitter, crying: When will this sun set? We would rather die with you than live under him! Xia's conduct is such that I must march against it. Join me, the One Man, in carrying out Heaven's sentence, and I will reward you richly. Do not doubt me—I keep my oath. Whoever breaks this oath I will execute, you and your families, without mercy. He proclaimed this to the host and composed the Oath of Tang. Tang declared himself strongly martial and took the title Martial King.

sentences.zh: 桀敗於有娀之虛，桀奔於鳴條，夏師敗績。

translations.literal: Jie was defeated at the ruins of Yousong; Jie fled to Mingtiao; Xia's army suffered rout.
translations.idiomatic: Jie was beaten at Yousong, fled to Mingtiao, and his army was routed.

sentences.zh: 湯遂伐三嵕，俘厥寶玉，義伯、仲伯作典寶。

translations.literal: Tang thereupon attacked Sanzong, captured its precious jade; Yibo and Zhongbo made "Canon of the Treasure."
translations.idiomatic: Tang then struck Sanzong, seized its sacred jades, and the lords Yibo and Zhongbo composed the Canon of the Treasure.

sentences.zh: 湯既勝夏，欲遷其社，不可，作夏社。

translations.literal: Tang having conquered Xia, wished to move its earth altar, could not, and made "Xia's Earth Altar."
translations.idiomatic: After conquering Xia, Tang wished to relocate its soil altar but could not, and composed Xia's Earth Altar.

sentences.zh: 伊尹報。

translations.literal: Yi Yin reported.
translations.idiomatic: Yi Yin made his report.

sentences.zh: 於是諸侯畢服，湯乃踐天子位，平定海內。

translations.literal: Thereupon the lords all submitted; Tang then trod the Son of Heaven position and pacified within the seas.
translations.idiomatic: The lords all submitted; Tang took the throne and brought peace inside the seas.

translations.literal: Jie was defeated at the ruins of Yousong; Jie fled to Mingtiao; Xia's army suffered rout. Tang thereupon attacked Sanzong, captured its precious jade; Yibo and Zhongbo made "Canon of the Treasure." Tang having conquered Xia, wished to move its earth altar, could not, and made "Xia's Earth Altar." Yi Yin reported. Thereupon the lords all submitted; Tang then trod the Son of Heaven position and pacified within the seas.
translations.idiomatic: Jie was beaten at Yousong, fled to Mingtiao, and his army was routed. Tang then struck Sanzong, seized its sacred jades, and the lords Yibo and Zhongbo composed the Canon of the Treasure. After conquering Xia, Tang wished to relocate its soil altar but could not, and composed Xia's Earth Altar. Yi Yin made his report. The lords all submitted; Tang took the throne and brought peace inside the seas.

sentences.zh: 湯歸至于泰卷陶，中壘作誥。

translations.literal: Tang returning reached Taijuan Tao; Zhonglei made announcements.
translations.idiomatic: On his return Tang halted at Taijuan Tao, where Zhonglei composed the announcements.

sentences.zh: 既絀夏命，還亳，作湯誥：「維三月，王自至於東郊。

translations.literal: Having ended Xia's mandate, he returned to Bo and made "Tang's Announcement": "It is the third month; the king personally reached the eastern suburbs."
translations.idiomatic: After ending Xia's mandate he returned to Bo and issued Tang's Announcement: In the third month the king came in person to the eastern suburbs.

sentences.zh: 告諸侯群后：『毋不有功於民，勤力乃事。

translations.literal: He told the lords and host of rulers: 'None shall fail to work for the people; exert yourselves in your tasks.
translations.idiomatic: He told the assembled rulers: None of you may neglect the people; labor diligently at your duties.

sentences.zh: 予乃大罰殛女，毋予怨。』

translations.literal: I will then greatly punish and kill you—do not blame me.'
translations.idiomatic: Or I will punish you severely—do not blame me for it.

sentences.zh: 曰：『古禹、皋陶久勞于外，其有功乎民，民乃有安。

translations.literal: Saying: 'Of old Yu and Gao Yao long toiled outside; they had merit toward the people; the people then had rest.
translations.idiomatic: He said: Long ago Yu and Gao Yao worked far from court; they served the people, and the people found peace.

sentences.zh: 東為江，北為濟，西為河，南為淮，四瀆已修，萬民乃有居。

translations.literal: East was the Yangtze, north was the Ji, west was the He, south was the Huai—the four channels were repaired; the myriad people then had dwellings.
translations.idiomatic: East lay the Long River, north the Ji, west the Yellow River, south the Huai—the four streams were tamed and the people had homes.

sentences.zh: 后稷降播，農殖百穀。

translations.literal: Prince Millet descended and sowed, farming planted the hundred grains.
translations.idiomatic: Hou Ji brought down seed and taught farming of every grain.

sentences.zh: 三公咸有功于民，故後有立。

translations.literal: The three dukes all had merit toward the people; therefore afterward they had establishment.
translations.idiomatic: The three high ministers all served the people, and their lines were founded in turn.

sentences.zh: 昔蚩尤與其大夫作亂百姓，帝乃弗予，有狀。

translations.literal: Of old Chiyou with his great officers made disorder among the hundred surnames; the Emperor then did not grant him—there was evidence.
translations.idiomatic: When Chiyou and his officers ravaged the people, Heaven withdrew favor—there was clear cause.

sentences.zh: 先王言不可不勉。』

translations.literal: The former kings' words must not go unheeded.'
translations.idiomatic: The words of the former kings must be heeded.

sentences.zh: 曰：『不道，毋之在國，女毋我怨。』」

translations.literal: Saying: 'If not the Way, do not let it remain in the state—you must not resent me.'
translations.idiomatic: He added: If you stray from the Way, you shall not remain in power—do not resent me for it.

sentences.zh: 以令諸侯。

translations.literal: Thereby commanding the lords.
translations.idiomatic: Thus he charged the regional lords.

sentences.zh: 伊尹作咸有一德，咎單作明居。

translations.literal: Yi Yin made "All Possess One Virtue"; Jiusdan made "Bright Dwelling."
translations.idiomatic: Yi Yin composed All Possess One Virtue; Jiusdan composed Bright Dwelling.

translations.literal: Tang returning reached Taijuan Tao; Zhonglei made announcements. Having ended Xia's mandate, he returned to Bo and made "Tang's Announcement": "It is the third month; the king personally reached the eastern suburbs." He told the lords and host of rulers: 'None shall fail to work for the people; exert yourselves in your tasks. I will then greatly punish and kill you—do not blame me.' Saying: 'Of old Yu and Gao Yao long toiled outside; they had merit toward the people; the people then had rest. East was the Yangtze, north was the Ji, west was the He, south was the Huai—the four channels were repaired; the myriad people then had dwellings. Prince Millet descended and sowed, farming planted the hundred grains. The three dukes all had merit toward the people; therefore afterward they had establishment. Of old Chiyou with his great officers made disorder among the hundred surnames; the Emperor then did not grant him—there was evidence. The former kings' words must not go unheeded.' Saying: 'If not the Way, do not let it remain in the state—you must not resent me.' Thereby commanding the lords. Yi Yin made "All Possess One Virtue"; Jiusdan made "Bright Dwelling."
translations.idiomatic: On his return Tang halted at Taijuan Tao, where Zhonglei composed the announcements. After ending Xia's mandate he returned to Bo and issued Tang's Announcement: In the third month the king came in person to the eastern suburbs. He told the assembled rulers: None of you may neglect the people; labor diligently at your duties. Or I will punish you severely—do not blame me for it. He said: Long ago Yu and Gao Yao worked far from court; they served the people, and the people found peace. East lay the Long River, north the Ji, west the Yellow River, south the Huai—the four streams were tamed and the people had homes. Hou Ji brought down seed and taught farming of every grain. The three high ministers all served the people, and their lines were founded in turn. When Chiyou and his officers ravaged the people, Heaven withdrew favor—there was clear cause. The words of the former kings must be heeded. He added: If you stray from the Way, you shall not remain in power—do not resent me for it. Thus he charged the regional lords. Yi Yin composed All Possess One Virtue; Jiusdan composed Bright Dwelling.

sentences.zh: 湯乃改正朔，易服色，上白，朝會以晝。

translations.literal: Tang thereupon changed the first month of the year, altered robe colors, honored white, and held court in daylight.
translations.idiomatic: Tang reformed the calendar, changed the court dress to favor white, and held audiences by day.

translations.literal: Tang thereupon changed the first month of the year, altered robe colors, honored white, and held court in daylight.
translations.idiomatic: Tang reformed the calendar, changed the court dress to favor white, and held audiences by day.

sentences.zh: 湯崩，太子太丁未立而卒，於是乃立太丁之弟外丙，是為帝外丙。

translations.literal: Tang passed away; heir son Taiding had not been established then died; thereupon they established Taiding's younger brother Waibing—this is Emperor Waibing.
translations.idiomatic: When Tang died, the heir Taiding died before he could rule, so Taiding's brother Waibing became Emperor Waibing.

sentences.zh: 帝外丙即位三年，崩，立外丙之弟中壬，是為帝中壬。

translations.literal: Emperor Waibing having assumed position three years, passed away; they established Waibing's younger brother Zhongren—this is Emperor Zhongren.
translations.idiomatic: Waibing ruled three years and died; his brother Zhongren succeeded as Emperor Zhongren.

sentences.zh: 帝中壬即位四年，崩，伊尹乃立太丁之子太甲。

translations.literal: Emperor Zhongren having assumed position four years, passed away; Yi Yin then established Taiding's son Taijia.
translations.idiomatic: Zhongren ruled four years and died; Yi Yin then set Taiding's son Taijia on the throne.

sentences.zh: 太甲，成湯適長孫也，是為帝太甲。

translations.literal: Taijia, Cheng Tang's eldest great-grandson by the proper line—this is Emperor Taijia.
translations.idiomatic: Taijia was Cheng Tang's senior great-grandson through the main line—Emperor Taijia.

sentences.zh: 帝太甲元年，伊尹作伊訓，作肆命，作徂后。

translations.literal: Emperor Taijia first year, Yi Yin made "Instructions for Taijia," "Loose Commands," and "Gone Kings."
translations.idiomatic: In Taijia's first year Yi Yin composed the Instructions for Taijia, the Loose Commands, and Gone Kings.

translations.literal: Tang passed away; heir son Taiding had not been established then died; thereupon they established Taiding's younger brother Waibing—this is Emperor Waibing. Emperor Waibing having assumed position three years, passed away; they established Waibing's younger brother Zhongren—this is Emperor Zhongren. Emperor Zhongren having assumed position four years, passed away; Yi Yin then established Taiding's son Taijia. Taijia, Cheng Tang's eldest great-grandson by the proper line—this is Emperor Taijia. Emperor Taijia first year, Yi Yin made "Instructions for Taijia," "Loose Commands," and "Gone Kings."
translations.idiomatic: When Tang died, the heir Taiding died before he could rule, so Taiding's brother Waibing became Emperor Waibing. Waibing ruled three years and died; his brother Zhongren succeeded as Emperor Zhongren. Zhongren ruled four years and died; Yi Yin then set Taiding's son Taijia on the throne. Taijia was Cheng Tang's senior great-grandson through the main line—Emperor Taijia. In Taijia's first year Yi Yin composed the Instructions for Taijia, the Loose Commands, and Gone Kings.

sentences.zh: 帝太甲既立三年，不明，暴虐，不遵湯法，亂德，於是伊尹放之於桐宮。

translations.literal: Emperor Taijia having been established three years, was not enlightened, cruel and tyrannical, did not follow Tang's laws, disordered virtue; thereupon Yi Yin banished him to Tong Palace.
translations.idiomatic: After three years Taijia proved dark, violent, and lawless; Yi Yin banished him to the Tong palace.

sentences.zh: 三年，伊尹攝行政當國，以朝諸侯。

translations.literal: Three years Yi Yin exercised government in proxy and held court over the lords.
translations.idiomatic: For three years Yi Yin ruled as regent and received the lords at court.

translations.literal: Emperor Taijia having been established three years, was not enlightened, cruel and tyrannical, did not follow Tang's laws, disordered virtue; thereupon Yi Yin banished him to Tong Palace. Three years Yi Yin exercised government in proxy and held court over the lords.
translations.idiomatic: After three years Taijia proved dark, violent, and lawless; Yi Yin banished him to the Tong palace. For three years Yi Yin ruled as regent and received the lords at court.

sentences.zh: 帝太甲居桐宮三年，悔過自責，反善，於是伊尹乃迎帝太甲而授之政。

translations.literal: Emperor Taijia dwelling in Tong Palace three years, repented fault and blamed himself, returned to good; thereupon Yi Yin then welcomed Emperor Taijia and handed him the government.
translations.idiomatic: Taijia spent three years in Tong, repented, and mended his ways; Yi Yin then brought him back and restored the government.

sentences.zh: 帝太甲修德，諸侯咸歸殷，百姓以寧。

translations.literal: Emperor Taijia cultivated virtue; the lords all returned to Yin; the hundred surnames thereby were tranquil.
translations.idiomatic: Taijia cultivated virtue; the lords rallied to Yin again and the people were calmed.

sentences.zh: 伊尹嘉之，乃作太甲訓三篇，褒帝太甲，稱太宗。

translations.literal: Yi Yin approved this, then made Taijia's Instructions in three pieces, praising Emperor Taijia, styling him Grand Ancestor.
translations.idiomatic: Yi Yin praised him, composed the three Instructions of Taijia, honored him as Grand Ancestor.

translations.literal: Emperor Taijia dwelling in Tong Palace three years, repented fault and blamed himself, returned to good; thereupon Yi Yin then welcomed Emperor Taijia and handed him the government. Emperor Taijia cultivated virtue; the lords all returned to Yin; the hundred surnames thereby were tranquil. Yi Yin approved this, then made Taijia's Instructions in three pieces, praising Emperor Taijia, styling him Grand Ancestor.
translations.idiomatic: Taijia spent three years in Tong, repented, and mended his ways; Yi Yin then brought him back and restored the government. Taijia cultivated virtue; the lords rallied to Yin again and the people were calmed. Yi Yin praised him, composed the three Instructions of Taijia, honored him as Grand Ancestor.

sentences.zh: 太宗崩，子沃丁立。

translations.literal: Grand Ancestor passed away; his son Woding established himself.
translations.idiomatic: When the Grand Ancestor died, his son Woding succeeded.

sentences.zh: 帝沃丁之時，伊尹卒。

translations.literal: In Emperor Woding's time, Yi Yin passed away.
translations.idiomatic: Under Woding, Yi Yin died.

sentences.zh: 既葬伊尹於亳，咎單遂訓伊尹事，作沃丁。

translations.literal: Having buried Yi Yin at Bo, Jiusdan then instructed on Yi Yin's affairs; he made "Woding."
translations.idiomatic: After Yi Yin was buried at Bo, Jiusdan expounded his deeds in the piece called Woding.

translations.literal: Grand Ancestor passed away; his son Woding established himself. In Emperor Woding's time, Yi Yin passed away. Having buried Yi Yin at Bo, Jiusdan then instructed on Yi Yin's affairs; he made "Woding."
translations.idiomatic: When the Grand Ancestor died, his son Woding succeeded. Under Woding, Yi Yin died. After Yi Yin was buried at Bo, Jiusdan expounded his deeds in the piece called Woding.

sentences.zh: 沃丁崩，弟太庚立，是為帝太庚。

translations.literal: Woding passed away; younger brother Taigeng established himself—this is Emperor Taigeng.
translations.idiomatic: Woding died; his brother Taigeng became Emperor Taigeng.

sentences.zh: 帝太庚崩，子帝小甲立。

translations.literal: Emperor Taigeng passed away; his son Emperor Xiaojia established himself.
translations.idiomatic: Taigeng died and his son Xiaojia ruled.

sentences.zh: 帝小甲崩，弟雍己立，是為帝雍己。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaojia passed away; younger brother Yongji established himself—this is Emperor Yongji.
translations.idiomatic: Xiaojia died; his brother Yongji became Emperor Yongji.

sentences.zh: 殷道衰，諸侯或不至。

translations.literal: Yin's way declined; some lords did not come to court.
translations.idiomatic: Yin's power waned and some lords stayed away from court.

translations.literal: Woding passed away; younger brother Taigeng established himself—this is Emperor Taigeng. Emperor Taigeng passed away; his son Emperor Xiaojia established himself. Emperor Xiaojia passed away; younger brother Yongji established himself—this is Emperor Yongji. Yin's way declined; some lords did not come to court.
translations.idiomatic: Woding died; his brother Taigeng became Emperor Taigeng. Taigeng died and his son Xiaojia ruled. Xiaojia died; his brother Yongji became Emperor Yongji. Yin's power waned and some lords stayed away from court.

sentences.zh: 帝雍己崩，弟太戊立，是為帝太戊。

translations.literal: Emperor Yongji passed away; younger brother Taiwu established himself—this is Emperor Taiwu.
translations.idiomatic: Yongji died; his brother Taiwu became Emperor Taiwu.

sentences.zh: 帝太戊立伊陟為相。

translations.literal: Emperor Taiwu established Yizhi as minister.
translations.idiomatic: Taiwu appointed Yizhi chief minister.

sentences.zh: 亳有祥桑谷共生於朝，一暮大拱。

translations.literal: At Bo there was an omen: mulberry and grain grew together in the court; in one night they became as thick as a clasped hand.
translations.idiomatic: At Bo mulberry and grain sprouted together in the courtyard and in one night grew thick as an arm.

sentences.zh: 帝太戊懼，問伊陟。

translations.literal: Emperor Taiwu feared and questioned Yizhi.
translations.idiomatic: Taiwu was frightened and asked Yizhi what it meant.

sentences.zh: 伊陟曰：「臣聞妖不勝德，帝之政其有闕與？

translations.literal: Yizhi said, "I have heard omens do not overcome virtue—perhaps the Emperor's government has gaps?
translations.idiomatic: Yizhi said: I have heard that portents cannot defeat virtue—perhaps your government needs mending?

sentences.zh: 帝其修德。」

translations.literal: The Emperor should cultivate virtue."
translations.idiomatic: Cultivate your virtue, my lord.

sentences.zh: 太戊從之，而祥桑枯死而去。

translations.literal: Taiwu followed it, and the auspicious mulberry withered and departed.
translations.idiomatic: Taiwu listened; the strange mulberry died away.

sentences.zh: 伊陟贊言于巫咸。

translations.literal: Yizhi praised the words to Wuxian.
translations.idiomatic: Yizhi relayed this counsel to Wuxian.

sentences.zh: 巫咸治王家有成，作咸艾，作太戊。

translations.literal: Wuxian ordered the royal house with success; he made "Salted Mugwort" and "Taiwu."
translations.idiomatic: Wuxian brought order to the royal house and composed Salted Mugwort and Taiwu.

sentences.zh: 帝太戊贊伊陟于廟，言弗臣，伊陟讓，作原命。

translations.literal: Emperor Taiwu praised Yizhi at the temple, saying he would not treat him as subject; Yizhi yielded; he made "Original Mandate."
translations.idiomatic: Taiwu honored Yizhi in the temple as more than a mere minister; Yizhi declined, and the Original Mandate was composed.

sentences.zh: 殷復興，諸侯歸之，故稱中宗。

translations.literal: Yin revived; the lords returned to it; therefore he is called Middle Ancestor.
translations.idiomatic: Yin flourished again and the lords rallied—hence he is called the Middle Ancestor.

translations.literal: Emperor Yongji passed away; younger brother Taiwu established himself—this is Emperor Taiwu. Emperor Taiwu established Yizhi as minister. At Bo there was an omen: mulberry and grain grew together in the court; in one night they became as thick as a clasped hand. Emperor Taiwu feared and questioned Yizhi. Yizhi said, "I have heard omens do not overcome virtue—perhaps the Emperor's government has gaps? The Emperor should cultivate virtue." Taiwu followed it, and the auspicious mulberry withered and departed. Yizhi praised the words to Wuxian. Wuxian ordered the royal house with success; he made "Salted Mugwort" and "Taiwu." Emperor Taiwu praised Yizhi at the temple, saying he would not treat him as subject; Yizhi yielded; he made "Original Mandate." Yin revived; the lords returned to it; therefore he is called Middle Ancestor.
translations.idiomatic: Yongji died; his brother Taiwu became Emperor Taiwu. Taiwu appointed Yizhi chief minister. At Bo mulberry and grain sprouted together in the courtyard and in one night grew thick as an arm. Taiwu was frightened and asked Yizhi what it meant. Yizhi said: I have heard that portents cannot defeat virtue—perhaps your government needs mending? Cultivate your virtue, my lord. Taiwu listened; the strange mulberry died away. Yizhi relayed this counsel to Wuxian. Wuxian brought order to the royal house and composed Salted Mugwort and Taiwu. Taiwu honored Yizhi in the temple as more than a mere minister; Yizhi declined, and the Original Mandate was composed. Yin flourished again and the lords rallied—hence he is called the Middle Ancestor.

sentences.zh: 中宗崩，子帝中丁立。

translations.literal: Middle Ancestor passed away; his son Emperor Zhongding established himself.
translations.idiomatic: When the Middle Ancestor died, his son Zhongding succeeded.

sentences.zh: 帝中丁遷于隞。

translations.literal: Emperor Zhongding moved the capital to Ao.
translations.idiomatic: Zhongding moved the capital to Ao.

sentences.zh: 河亶甲居相。

translations.literal: Hedanjia dwelt at Xiang.
translations.idiomatic: Hedanjia ruled from Xiang.

sentences.zh: 祖乙遷于邢。

translations.literal: Zuyi moved the capital to Xing.
translations.idiomatic: Zuyi moved the seat to Xing.

sentences.zh: 帝中丁崩，弟外壬立，是為帝外壬。

translations.literal: Emperor Zhongding passed away; younger brother Wairen established himself—this is Emperor Wairen.
translations.idiomatic: Zhongding died; his brother Wairen became Emperor Wairen.

sentences.zh: 仲丁書闕不具。

translations.literal: The Zhongding annals are defective and incomplete.
translations.idiomatic: The records of Zhongding are lost and incomplete.

sentences.zh: 帝外壬崩，弟河亶甲立，是為帝河亶甲。

translations.literal: Emperor Wairen passed away; younger brother Hedanjia established himself—this is Emperor Hedanjia.
translations.idiomatic: Wairen died; his brother Hedanjia became Emperor Hedanjia.

sentences.zh: 河亶甲時，殷復衰。

translations.literal: In Hedanjia's time, Yin again declined.
translations.idiomatic: Under Hedanjia Yin weakened again.

translations.literal: Middle Ancestor passed away; his son Emperor Zhongding established himself. Emperor Zhongding moved the capital to Ao. Hedanjia dwelt at Xiang. Zuyi moved the capital to Xing. Emperor Zhongding passed away; younger brother Wairen established himself—this is Emperor Wairen. The Zhongding annals are defective and incomplete. Emperor Wairen passed away; younger brother Hedanjia established himself—this is Emperor Hedanjia. In Hedanjia's time, Yin again declined.
translations.idiomatic: When the Middle Ancestor died, his son Zhongding succeeded. Zhongding moved the capital to Ao. Hedanjia ruled from Xiang. Zuyi moved the seat to Xing. Zhongding died; his brother Wairen became Emperor Wairen. The records of Zhongding are lost and incomplete. Wairen died; his brother Hedanjia became Emperor Hedanjia. Under Hedanjia Yin weakened again.

sentences.zh: 河亶甲崩，子帝祖乙立。

translations.literal: Hedanjia passed away; his son Emperor Zuyi established himself.
translations.idiomatic: Hedanjia died and his son Zuyi ruled.

sentences.zh: 帝祖乙立，殷復興。

translations.literal: Emperor Zuyi having been established, Yin again revived.
translations.idiomatic: When Zuyi came to the throne, Yin revived once more.

sentences.zh: 巫賢任職。

translations.literal: Wuxian held office.
translations.idiomatic: Wuxian the shaman served in office.

translations.literal: Hedanjia passed away; his son Emperor Zuyi established himself. Emperor Zuyi having been established, Yin again revived. Wuxian held office.
translations.idiomatic: Hedanjia died and his son Zuyi ruled. When Zuyi came to the throne, Yin revived once more. Wuxian the shaman served in office.

sentences.zh: 祖乙崩，子帝祖辛立。

translations.literal: Zuyi passed away; his son Emperor Zuxin established himself.
translations.idiomatic: Zuyi died and his son Zuxin ruled.

sentences.zh: 帝祖辛崩，弟沃甲立，是為帝沃甲。

translations.literal: Emperor Zuxin passed away; younger brother Wojia established himself—this is Emperor Wojia.
translations.idiomatic: Zuxin died; his brother Wojia became Emperor Wojia.

sentences.zh: 帝沃甲崩，立沃甲兄祖辛之子祖丁，是為帝祖丁。

translations.literal: Emperor Wojia passed away; they established Wojia's elder brother Zuxin's son Zuding—this is Emperor Zuding.
translations.idiomatic: Wojia died; Zuxin's son Zuding, Wojia's nephew, became Emperor Zuding.

sentences.zh: 帝祖丁崩，立弟沃甲之子南庚，是為帝南庚。

translations.literal: Emperor Zuding passed away; they established younger brother Wojia's son Nangeng—this is Emperor Nangeng.
translations.idiomatic: Zuding died; Wojia's son Nangeng succeeded as Emperor Nangeng.

sentences.zh: 帝南庚崩，立帝祖丁之子陽甲，是為帝陽甲。

translations.literal: Emperor Nangeng passed away; they established Emperor Zuding's son Yangjia—this is Emperor Yangjia.
translations.idiomatic: Nangeng died; Zuding's son Yangjia became Emperor Yangjia.

sentences.zh: 帝陽甲之時，殷衰。

translations.literal: In Emperor Yangjia's time, Yin declined.
translations.idiomatic: Under Yangjia Yin declined again.

translations.literal: Zuyi passed away; his son Emperor Zuxin established himself. Emperor Zuxin passed away; younger brother Wojia established himself—this is Emperor Wojia. Emperor Wojia passed away; they established Wojia's elder brother Zuxin's son Zuding—this is Emperor Zuding. Emperor Zuding passed away; they established younger brother Wojia's son Nangeng—this is Emperor Nangeng. Emperor Nangeng passed away; they established Emperor Zuding's son Yangjia—this is Emperor Yangjia. In Emperor Yangjia's time, Yin declined.
translations.idiomatic: Zuyi died and his son Zuxin ruled. Zuxin died; his brother Wojia became Emperor Wojia. Wojia died; Zuxin's son Zuding, Wojia's nephew, became Emperor Zuding. Zuding died; Wojia's son Nangeng succeeded as Emperor Nangeng. Nangeng died; Zuding's son Yangjia became Emperor Yangjia. Under Yangjia Yin declined again.

sentences.zh: 自中丁以來，廢適而更立諸弟子，弟子或爭相代立，比九世亂，於是諸侯莫朝。

translations.literal: From Zhongding onward they cast aside the heir-apparent and repeatedly established younger sons of the line; younger sons sometimes struggled to replace one another—nine generations in disorder; thereupon the lords none came to court.
translations.idiomatic: From Zhongding on the rightful heir was set aside for younger brothers, who fought for the throne—nine reigns of chaos—and the lords ceased to attend court.

translations.literal: From Zhongding onward they cast aside the heir-apparent and repeatedly established younger sons of the line; younger sons sometimes struggled to replace one another—nine generations in disorder; thereupon the lords none came to court.
translations.idiomatic: From Zhongding on the rightful heir was set aside for younger brothers, who fought for the throne—nine reigns of chaos—and the lords ceased to attend court.

sentences.zh: 帝陽甲崩，弟盤庚立，是為帝盤庚。

translations.literal: Emperor Yangjia passed away; younger brother Pangeng established himself—this is Emperor Pangeng.
translations.idiomatic: Yangjia died; his brother Pangeng became Emperor Pangeng.

sentences.zh: 帝盤庚之時，殷已都河北，盤庚渡河南，復居成湯之故居，乃五遷，無定處。

translations.literal: In Emperor Pangeng's time, Yin had already placed its capital north of the He; Pangeng crossed south of the He, again dwelt in Cheng Tang's old residence—thus five migrations, no fixed place.
translations.idiomatic: Yin had already sat north of the Yellow River; Pangeng crossed south to Cheng Tang's old capital—five moves in all, never long in one place.

sentences.zh: 殷民咨胥皆怨，不欲徙。

translations.literal: Yin people consulted and murmured all resentful, not wishing to move.
translations.idiomatic: The people of Yin grumbled and refused to move.

sentences.zh: 盤庚乃告諭諸侯大臣曰：「昔高后成湯與爾之先祖俱定天下，法則可修。

translations.literal: Pangeng thereupon announced to the lords and great ministers, saying, "Of old the High Queen Cheng Tang with your ancestors together settled all under Heaven; laws and models can be cultivated.
translations.idiomatic: Pangeng addressed the lords and ministers: Long ago High Tang and your forebears founded the realm together—its laws can still be honored.

sentences.zh: 捨而弗勉，何以成德！」

translations.literal: Cast them aside and fail to exert yourselves—how complete virtue!"
translations.idiomatic: If you cast them aside and slacken, how will virtue be perfected?

sentences.zh: 乃遂涉河南，治亳，行湯之政，然後百姓由寧，殷道復興。

translations.literal: Thereupon he crossed south of the He, governed Bo, practiced Tang's government; then the hundred surnames thereby were tranquil; Yin's way again revived.
translations.idiomatic: He crossed the river, ruled from Bo, restored Tang's policies, and the people found rest as Yin rose again.

sentences.zh: 諸侯來朝，以其遵成湯之德也。

translations.literal: The lords came to court, because they followed Cheng Tang's virtue.
translations.idiomatic: The lords returned to court because he followed Tang's example.

translations.literal: Emperor Yangjia passed away; younger brother Pangeng established himself—this is Emperor Pangeng. In Emperor Pangeng's time, Yin had already placed its capital north of the He; Pangeng crossed south of the He, again dwelt in Cheng Tang's old residence—thus five migrations, no fixed place. Yin people consulted and murmured all resentful, not wishing to move. Pangeng thereupon announced to the lords and great ministers, saying, "Of old the High Queen Cheng Tang with your ancestors together settled all under Heaven; laws and models can be cultivated. Cast them aside and fail to exert yourselves—how complete virtue!" Thereupon he crossed south of the He, governed Bo, practiced Tang's government; then the hundred surnames thereby were tranquil; Yin's way again revived. The lords came to court, because they followed Cheng Tang's virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Yangjia died; his brother Pangeng became Emperor Pangeng. Yin had already sat north of the Yellow River; Pangeng crossed south to Cheng Tang's old capital—five moves in all, never long in one place. The people of Yin grumbled and refused to move. Pangeng addressed the lords and ministers: Long ago High Tang and your forebears founded the realm together—its laws can still be honored. If you cast them aside and slacken, how will virtue be perfected? He crossed the river, ruled from Bo, restored Tang's policies, and the people found rest as Yin rose again. The lords returned to court because he followed Tang's example.

sentences.zh: 帝盤庚崩，弟小辛立，是為帝小辛。

translations.literal: Emperor Pangeng passed away; younger brother Xiaoxin established himself—this is Emperor Xiaoxin.
translations.idiomatic: Pangeng died; his brother Xiaoxin became Emperor Xiaoxin.

sentences.zh: 帝小辛立，殷復衰。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaoxin having been established, Yin again declined.
translations.idiomatic: Under Xiaoxin Yin weakened again.

sentences.zh: 百姓思盤庚，乃作盤庚三篇。

translations.literal: The hundred surnames missed Pangeng; they then made Pangeng in three pieces.
translations.idiomatic: The people longed for Pangeng, and the three chapters of Pangeng were composed.

sentences.zh: 帝小辛崩，弟小乙立，是為帝小乙。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaoxin passed away; younger brother Xiaoyi established himself—this is Emperor Xiaoyi.
translations.idiomatic: Xiaoxin died; his brother Xiaoyi succeeded.

translations.literal: Emperor Pangeng passed away; younger brother Xiaoxin established himself—this is Emperor Xiaoxin. Emperor Xiaoxin having been established, Yin again declined. The hundred surnames missed Pangeng; they then made Pangeng in three pieces. Emperor Xiaoxin passed away; younger brother Xiaoyi established himself—this is Emperor Xiaoyi.
translations.idiomatic: Pangeng died; his brother Xiaoxin became Emperor Xiaoxin. Under Xiaoxin Yin weakened again. The people longed for Pangeng, and the three chapters of Pangeng were composed. Xiaoxin died; his brother Xiaoyi succeeded.

sentences.zh: 帝小乙崩，子帝武丁立。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaoyi passed away; his son Emperor Wuding established himself.
translations.idiomatic: Xiaoyi died and his son Wuding ruled.

sentences.zh: 帝武丁即位，思復興殷，而未得其佐。

translations.literal: Emperor Wuding having assumed the throne, thought to revive Yin but had not yet found his helper.
translations.idiomatic: Wuding wished to restore Yin but had not yet found the right minister.

sentences.zh: 三年不言，政事決定於冢宰，以觀國風。

translations.literal: Three years he did not speak; government decisions lay with the grand steward, to observe the state's temper.
translations.idiomatic: For three years he held his tongue while the chief minister decided affairs, the better to read the realm.

sentences.zh: 武丁夜夢得聖人，名曰說。

translations.literal: Wuding night dreamed he obtained a sage, named Yue.
translations.idiomatic: One night Wuding dreamed of a sage named Yue.

sentences.zh: 以夢所見視群臣百吏，皆非也。

translations.literal: According to the dream's appearance he viewed ministers and hundred clerks—none matched.
translations.idiomatic: He compared the vision to his officers—none fit the dream.

sentences.zh: 於是乃使百工營求之野，得說於傅險中。

translations.literal: Thereupon he sent craftsmen to seek in the wilds and obtained Yue at Fu ravine.
translations.idiomatic: He sent artisans to search the wilds and found Yue at Fu ravine.

sentences.zh: 是時說為胥靡，筑於傅險。

translations.literal: At that time Yue was a convict laborer, building at Fu ravine.
translations.idiomatic: Yue was then a corvée laborer working the cliffs at Fu.

sentences.zh: 見於武丁，武丁曰是也。

translations.literal: Presented to Wuding, Wuding said this is he.
translations.idiomatic: When he was brought to Wuding, the king said, This is the man.

sentences.zh: 得而與之語，果聖人，舉以為相，殷國大治。

translations.literal: Having obtained him and conversed with him, he was indeed a sage; raised to be minister—Yin state was greatly ordered.
translations.idiomatic: They spoke and found him a sage; he was made minister and Yin was well governed.

sentences.zh: 故遂以傅險姓之，號曰傅說。

translations.literal: Therefore they took Fu ravine as his surname, styled Fu Yue.
translations.idiomatic: He took his name from Fu ravine and was called Fu Yue.

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaoyi passed away; his son Emperor Wuding established himself. Emperor Wuding having assumed the throne, thought to revive Yin but had not yet found his helper. Three years he did not speak; government decisions lay with the grand steward, to observe the state's temper. Wuding night dreamed he obtained a sage, named Yue. According to the dream's appearance he viewed ministers and hundred clerks—none matched. Thereupon he sent craftsmen to seek in the wilds and obtained Yue at Fu ravine. At that time Yue was a convict laborer, building at Fu ravine. Presented to Wuding, Wuding said this is he. Having obtained him and conversed with him, he was indeed a sage; raised to be minister—Yin state was greatly ordered. Therefore they took Fu ravine as his surname, styled Fu Yue.
translations.idiomatic: Xiaoyi died and his son Wuding ruled. Wuding wished to restore Yin but had not yet found the right minister. For three years he held his tongue while the chief minister decided affairs, the better to read the realm. One night Wuding dreamed of a sage named Yue. He compared the vision to his officers—none fit the dream. He sent artisans to search the wilds and found Yue at Fu ravine. Yue was then a corvée laborer working the cliffs at Fu. When he was brought to Wuding, the king said, This is the man. They spoke and found him a sage; he was made minister and Yin was well governed. He took his name from Fu ravine and was called Fu Yue.

sentences.zh: 帝武丁祭成湯，明日，有飛雉登鼎耳而呴，武丁懼。

translations.literal: Emperor Wuding sacrificed to Cheng Tang; next day a flying pheasant perched on the tripod ear and crowed; Wuding feared.
translations.idiomatic: Wuding sacrificed to Tang; the next day a pheasant lit on the cauldron's ear and cried; the king was afraid.

sentences.zh: 祖己曰：「王勿憂，先修政事。」

translations.literal: Zuji said, "King do not worry—first cultivate government affairs."
translations.idiomatic: Zuji said: Do not fear, my king—set your government in order first.

sentences.zh: 祖己乃訓王曰：「唯天監下典厥義，降年有永有不永，非天夭民，中絕其命。

translations.literal: Zuji thereupon instructed the king, saying, "Only Heaven oversees below, grounding its righteousness; it sends down years long and not long—it is not Heaven cutting short the people's lives; mid-way it cuts off their mandate.
translations.idiomatic: Zuji lectured the king: Heaven looks down and grounds its justice in right conduct; it grants long or short life—not to cut men off in youth but to end those who stray mid-course.

sentences.zh: 民有不若德，不聽罪，天既附命正厥德，乃曰其奈何。

translations.literal: When the people lack virtue fitting them, refuse to hear their guilt, Heaven having attached mandate corrects their virtue—then it says what can be done.
translations.idiomatic: When men lack fitting virtue and ignore their faults, Heaven attaches a charge to straighten their conduct—then we ask what is to be done.

sentences.zh: 鳴呼！

translations.literal: Alas!
translations.idiomatic: Alas!

sentences.zh: 王嗣敬民，罔非天繼，常祀毋禮于棄道。」

translations.literal: King's heir reverently serve the people—none is not Heaven's succession; in regular sacrifices do not be unobservant in the way of casting aside the Way.
translations.idiomatic: The king's heir must honor the people—all live under Heaven's succession; in your regular rites never carelessly abandon the Way.

sentences.zh: 武丁修政行德，天下咸驩，殷道復興。

translations.literal: Wuding cultivated government and practiced virtue; all under Heaven rejoiced; Yin's way again revived.
translations.idiomatic: Wuding set his house in order and practiced virtue; the realm rejoiced and Yin flourished again.

translations.literal: Emperor Wuding sacrificed to Cheng Tang; next day a flying pheasant perched on the tripod ear and crowed; Wuding feared. Zuji said, "King do not worry—first cultivate government affairs." Zuji thereupon instructed the king, saying, "Only Heaven oversees below, grounding its righteousness; it sends down years long and not long—it is not Heaven cutting short the people's lives; mid-way it cuts off their mandate. When the people lack virtue fitting them, refuse to hear their guilt, Heaven having attached mandate corrects their virtue—then it says what can be done. Alas! King's heir reverently serve the people—none is not Heaven's succession; in regular sacrifices do not be unobservant in the way of casting aside the Way. Wuding cultivated government and practiced virtue; all under Heaven rejoiced; Yin's way again revived.
translations.idiomatic: Wuding sacrificed to Tang; the next day a pheasant lit on the cauldron's ear and cried; the king was afraid. Zuji said: Do not fear, my king—set your government in order first. Zuji lectured the king: Heaven looks down and grounds its justice in right conduct; it grants long or short life—not to cut men off in youth but to end those who stray mid-course. When men lack fitting virtue and ignore their faults, Heaven attaches a charge to straighten their conduct—then we ask what is to be done. Alas! The king's heir must honor the people—all live under Heaven's succession; in your regular rites never carelessly abandon the Way. Wuding set his house in order and practiced virtue; the realm rejoiced and Yin flourished again.

sentences.zh: 帝武丁崩，子帝祖庚立。

translations.literal: Emperor Wuding passed away; his son Emperor Zugeng established himself.
translations.idiomatic: Wuding died and his son Zugeng succeeded.

sentences.zh: 祖己嘉武丁之以祥雉為德，立其廟為高宗，遂作高宗肜日及訓。

translations.literal: Zuji approved Wuding's taking the auspicious pheasant as virtue, established his temple as Gaozong, thereupon made Gaozong's Congratulatory Day and Instructions.
translations.idiomatic: Zuji praised Wuding for turning the pheasant omen to good; his temple was titled Gaozong, and the Congratulatory Day of Gaozong and related texts were composed.

translations.literal: Emperor Wuding passed away; his son Emperor Zugeng established himself. Zuji approved Wuding's taking the auspicious pheasant as virtue, established his temple as Gaozong, thereupon made Gaozong's Congratulatory Day and Instructions.
translations.idiomatic: Wuding died and his son Zugeng succeeded. Zuji praised Wuding for turning the pheasant omen to good; his temple was titled Gaozong, and the Congratulatory Day of Gaozong and related texts were composed.

sentences.zh: 帝祖庚崩，弟祖甲立，是為帝甲。

translations.literal: Emperor Zugeng passed away; younger brother Zujia established himself—this is Emperor Jia.
translations.idiomatic: Zugeng died; his brother Zujia became Emperor Jia.

sentences.zh: 帝甲淫亂，殷復衰。

translations.literal: Emperor Jia dissolute and chaotic, Yin again declined.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Jia gave himself to vice and Yin declined again.

translations.literal: Emperor Zugeng passed away; younger brother Zujia established himself—this is Emperor Jia. Emperor Jia dissolute and chaotic, Yin again declined.
translations.idiomatic: Zugeng died; his brother Zujia became Emperor Jia. Emperor Jia gave himself to vice and Yin declined again.

sentences.zh: 帝甲崩，子帝廩辛立。

translations.literal: Emperor Jia passed away; his son Emperor Linxin established himself.
translations.idiomatic: Jia died and his son Linxin ruled.

sentences.zh: 帝廩辛崩，弟庚丁立，是為帝庚丁。

translations.literal: Emperor Linxin passed away; younger brother Gengding established himself—this is Emperor Gengding.
translations.idiomatic: Linxin died; his brother Gengding succeeded.

sentences.zh: 帝庚丁崩，子帝武乙立。

translations.literal: Emperor Gengding passed away; his son Emperor Wuyi established himself.
translations.idiomatic: Gengding died and his son Wuyi ruled.

sentences.zh: 殷復去亳，徙河北。

translations.literal: Yin again left Bo, moved north of the He.
translations.idiomatic: Yin again abandoned Bo and moved north of the Yellow River.

translations.literal: Emperor Jia passed away; his son Emperor Linxin established himself. Emperor Linxin passed away; younger brother Gengding established himself—this is Emperor Gengding. Emperor Gengding passed away; his son Emperor Wuyi established himself. Yin again left Bo, moved north of the He.
translations.idiomatic: Jia died and his son Linxin ruled. Linxin died; his brother Gengding succeeded. Gengding died and his son Wuyi ruled. Yin again abandoned Bo and moved north of the Yellow River.

sentences.zh: 帝武乙無道，為偶人，謂之天神。

translations.literal: Emperor Wuyi lacked the Way; he made a puppet and called it Heaven's Spirit.
translations.idiomatic: Wuyi was lawless: he fashioned a straw figure and called it the god of Heaven.

sentences.zh: 與之博，令人為行。

translations.literal: He gambled with it, having men act the moves.
translations.idiomatic: He played dice against it while others moved the pieces.

sentences.zh: 天神不勝，乃僇辱之。

translations.literal: When Heaven's Spirit did not win, he then humiliated and shamed it.
translations.idiomatic: When the dummy lost, he had it mocked and disgraced.

sentences.zh: 為革囊，盛血，卬而射之，命曰「射天」。

translations.literal: He made a leather bag, filled it with blood, raised and shot it, naming it "shooting Heaven."
translations.idiomatic: He filled a leather sack with blood, hung it up, shot it, and called the game shooting Heaven.

sentences.zh: 武乙獵於河渭之閒，暴雷，武乙震死。

translations.literal: Wuyi hunted between the He and Wei; violent thunder struck; Wuyi was killed by lightning.
translations.idiomatic: Wuyi hunted between the Yellow and Wei rivers; a thunderbolt struck and killed him.

sentences.zh: 子帝太丁立。

translations.literal: His son Emperor Taiding established himself.
translations.idiomatic: His son Taiding succeeded him.

sentences.zh: 帝太丁崩，子帝乙立。

translations.literal: Emperor Taiding passed away; his son Emperor Yi established himself.
translations.idiomatic: Taiding died and his son Yi ruled.

sentences.zh: 帝乙立，殷益衰。

translations.literal: Emperor Yi having been established, Yin further declined.
translations.idiomatic: Under Yi Yin declined further.

translations.literal: Emperor Wuyi lacked the Way; he made a puppet and called it Heaven's Spirit. He gambled with it, having men act the moves. When Heaven's Spirit did not win, he then humiliated and shamed it. He made a leather bag, filled it with blood, raised and shot it, naming it "shooting Heaven." Wuyi hunted between the He and Wei; violent thunder struck; Wuyi was killed by lightning. His son Emperor Taiding established himself. Emperor Taiding passed away; his son Emperor Yi established himself. Emperor Yi having been established, Yin further declined.
translations.idiomatic: Wuyi was lawless: he fashioned a straw figure and called it the god of Heaven. He played dice against it while others moved the pieces. When the dummy lost, he had it mocked and disgraced. He filled a leather sack with blood, hung it up, shot it, and called the game shooting Heaven. Wuyi hunted between the Yellow and Wei rivers; a thunderbolt struck and killed him. His son Taiding succeeded him. Taiding died and his son Yi ruled. Under Yi Yin declined further.

sentences.zh: 帝乙長子曰微子啟，啟母賤，不得嗣。

translations.literal: Emperor Yi's eldest son was Weizi Qi; Qi's mother was lowly—he could not succeed.
translations.idiomatic: Yi's eldest son was Weizi Qi, but Qi's mother was of low rank so he could not inherit.

sentences.zh: 少子辛，辛母正后，辛為嗣。

translations.literal: Younger son Xin; Xin's mother was the proper queen—Xin was heir.
translations.idiomatic: The younger son Xin had a queen mother and became heir.

sentences.zh: 帝乙崩，子辛立，是為帝辛，天下謂之紂。

translations.literal: Emperor Yi passed away; his son Xin established himself—this is Emperor Xin; all under Heaven called him Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: When Yi died, Xin ruled as Emperor Xin—the man the world calls Zhou.

translations.literal: Emperor Yi's eldest son was Weizi Qi; Qi's mother was lowly—he could not succeed. Younger son Xin; Xin's mother was the proper queen—Xin was heir. Emperor Yi passed away; his son Xin established himself—this is Emperor Xin; all under Heaven called him Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: Yi's eldest son was Weizi Qi, but Qi's mother was of low rank so he could not inherit. The younger son Xin had a queen mother and became heir. When Yi died, Xin ruled as Emperor Xin—the man the world calls Zhou.

sentences.zh: 帝紂資辨捷疾，聞見甚敏；

translations.literal: Emperor Zhou was quick of wit and tongue, keen of ear and eye;
translations.idiomatic: Zhou was clever, eloquent, and sharp of sense;

sentences.zh: 材力過人，手格猛獸；

translations.literal: his strength surpassed others, his hands could grapple fierce beasts;
translations.idiomatic: his strength exceeded other men and he could wrestle wild beasts with his bare hands;

sentences.zh: 知足以距諫，言足以飾非；

translations.literal: his knowledge enough to reject remonstrance, his words enough to gloss fault;
translations.idiomatic: his wits let him brush off advice and his tongue varnish any fault;

sentences.zh: 矜人臣以能，高天下以聲，以為皆出己之下。

translations.literal: he boasted to ministers of his ability, raised himself above the realm in reputation, thinking all came out below himself.
translations.idiomatic: he flaunted his talents to his ministers, looked down on the realm, and fancied none his equal.

sentences.zh: 好酒淫樂，嬖於婦人。

translations.literal: He loved wine and dissolute music, doted on women.
translations.idiomatic: He drowned in wine and lewd music and doted on women.

sentences.zh: 愛妲己，妲己之言是從。

translations.literal: He loved Daji; whatever Daji said he followed.
translations.idiomatic: He adored Daji and obeyed her every word.

sentences.zh: 於是使師涓作新淫聲，北里之舞，靡靡之樂。

translations.literal: Thereupon he had Master Juan make new licentious sounds, northern quarter dances, dissolving music.
translations.idiomatic: He had Master Juan compose decadent airs, dances of the northern lanes, and enervating tunes.

sentences.zh: 厚賦稅以實鹿臺之錢，而盈鉅橋之粟。

translations.literal: He increased taxes to fill Lutai's coin and overflow Juliao's grain.
translations.idiomatic: He piled taxes to stock Lutai with gold and Juliao with grain.

sentences.zh: 益收狗馬奇物，充仞宮室。

translations.literal: He further gathered dogs, horses, and curiosities, filling the palace chambers.
translations.idiomatic: He gathered dogs, horses, and rarities until the palaces burst.

sentences.zh: 益廣沙丘苑臺，多取野獸蜚鳥置其中。

translations.literal: He further enlarged the Shaqiu park and tower, taking many wild beasts and flying birds to place within.
translations.idiomatic: He widened the Shaqiu hunting park and tower and filled them with beasts and birds.

sentences.zh: 慢於鬼神。

translations.literal: He was negligent toward ghosts and spirits.
translations.idiomatic: He neglected the ghosts and spirits.

sentences.zh: 大聚樂戲於沙丘，以酒為池，縣肉為林，使男女裸相逐其閒，為長夜之飲。

translations.literal: He greatly gathered music and games at Shaqiu, made a pool of wine, hung meat as a forest, had men and women chase each other naked among them, drinking the long night.
translations.idiomatic: He staged revels at Shaqiu with a lake of wine, a forest of hanging meat, naked men and women sporting between them, and feasts that lasted the night through.

translations.literal: Emperor Zhou was quick of wit and tongue, keen of ear and eye; his strength surpassed others, his hands could grapple fierce beasts; his knowledge enough to reject remonstrance, his words enough to gloss fault; he boasted to ministers of his ability, raised himself above the realm in reputation, thinking all came out below himself. He loved wine and dissolute music, doted on women. He loved Daji; whatever Daji said he followed. Thereupon he had Master Juan make new licentious sounds, northern quarter dances, dissolving music. He increased taxes to fill Lutai's coin and overflow Juliao's grain. He further gathered dogs, horses, and curiosities, filling the palace chambers. He further enlarged the Shaqiu park and tower, taking many wild beasts and flying birds to place within. He was negligent toward ghosts and spirits. He greatly gathered music and games at Shaqiu, made a pool of wine, hung meat as a forest, had men and women chase each other naked among them, drinking the long night.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou was clever, eloquent, and sharp of sense; his strength exceeded other men and he could wrestle wild beasts with his bare hands; his wits let him brush off advice and his tongue varnish any fault; he flaunted his talents to his ministers, looked down on the realm, and fancied none his equal. He drowned in wine and lewd music and doted on women. He adored Daji and obeyed her every word. He had Master Juan compose decadent airs, dances of the northern lanes, and enervating tunes. He piled taxes to stock Lutai with gold and Juliao with grain. He gathered dogs, horses, and rarities until the palaces burst. He widened the Shaqiu hunting park and tower and filled them with beasts and birds. He neglected the ghosts and spirits. He staged revels at Shaqiu with a lake of wine, a forest of hanging meat, naked men and women sporting between them, and feasts that lasted the night through.

sentences.zh: 百姓怨望而諸侯有畔者，於是紂乃重刑辟，有炮格之法。

translations.literal: The hundred surnames resented while lords had rebels; thereupon Zhou then doubled harsh punishments, having the paoge method.
translations.idiomatic: The people muttered and lords rebelled; Zhou answered with crueler laws and the paoge torture.

sentences.zh: 以西伯昌、九侯、鄂侯為三公。

translations.literal: He made Western Earl Chang, the Nine Marquises, and the Marquis of E the three dukes.
translations.idiomatic: He named Western Earl Chang, the Nine Marquises, and the Marquis of E as the three high dukes.

sentences.zh: 九侯有好女，入之紂。

translations.literal: The Nine Marquises had a beautiful daughter; she was sent in to Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: The Nine Marquises offered a beautiful daughter to the court.

sentences.zh: 九侯女不喜淫，紂怒，殺之，而醢九侯。

translations.literal: The Nine Marquises' daughter did not love dissipation; Zhou in anger killed her and minced the Nine Marquises.
translations.idiomatic: She disliked debauchery; Zhou killed her in fury and had the Nine Marquises ground to paste.

sentences.zh: 鄂侯爭之彊，辨之疾，并脯鄂侯。

translations.literal: The Marquis of E disputed it strongly, argued it swiftly; together he pickled the Marquis of E.
translations.idiomatic: The Marquis of E remonstrated fiercely; Zhou had him pickled as well.

sentences.zh: 西伯昌聞之，竊嘆。

translations.literal: Western Earl Chang hearing it sighed in secret.
translations.idiomatic: When Western Earl Chang heard this he sighed in private.

sentences.zh: 崇侯虎知之，以告紂，紂囚西伯羑里。

translations.literal: Chonghou Hu knew it, reported to Zhou; Zhou imprisoned Western Earl at Youli.
translations.idiomatic: Chonghou Hu informed on him, and Zhou jailed the Western Earl at Youli.

sentences.zh: 西伯之臣閎夭之徒，求美女奇物善馬以獻紂，紂乃赦西伯。

translations.literal: Western Earl's ministers Hongyao and others sought beauties, curiosities, and fine horses to present to Zhou; Zhou then pardoned Western Earl.
translations.idiomatic: His men Hongyao and the rest ransomed him with beauties, treasures, and horses, and Zhou set him free.

sentences.zh: 西伯出而獻洛西之地，以請除炮格之刑。

translations.literal: Western Earl coming out presented the lands west of the Luo, requesting removal of the paoge punishment.
translations.idiomatic: On his release he ceded the lands west of the Luo to buy the abolition of paoge.

sentences.zh: 紂乃許之，賜弓矢斧鉞，使得征伐，為西伯。

translations.literal: Zhou then granted it, gave bow, arrows, axe, and halberd, allowing him to campaign, making him Western Earl.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou agreed and gave him bow, axe, and halberd with authority to wage war as Western Earl.

sentences.zh: 而用費中為政。

translations.literal: Yet he employed Fei Zhong to govern.
translations.idiomatic: Yet he put Fei Zhong in charge of government.

sentences.zh: 費中善諛，好利，殷人弗親。

translations.literal: Fei Zhong was skilled at flattery, loved profit—Yin people did not cleave to him.
translations.idiomatic: Fei Zhong flattered and grasped after gain; the Yin people did not trust him.

sentences.zh: 紂又用惡來。

translations.literal: Zhou again employed E Lai.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou also employed E Lai.

sentences.zh: 惡來善毀讒，諸侯以此益疏。

translations.literal: E Lai was skilled at slander; by this the lords grew more distant.
translations.idiomatic: E Lai excelled at malice; the lords drew farther away.

translations.literal: The hundred surnames resented while lords had rebels; thereupon Zhou then doubled harsh punishments, having the paoge method. He made Western Earl Chang, the Nine Marquises, and the Marquis of E the three dukes. The Nine Marquises had a beautiful daughter; she was sent in to Zhou. The Nine Marquises' daughter did not love dissipation; Zhou in anger killed her and minced the Nine Marquises. The Marquis of E disputed it strongly, argued it swiftly; together he pickled the Marquis of E. Western Earl Chang hearing it sighed in secret. Chonghou Hu knew it, reported to Zhou; Zhou imprisoned Western Earl at Youli. Western Earl's ministers Hongyao and others sought beauties, curiosities, and fine horses to present to Zhou; Zhou then pardoned Western Earl. Western Earl coming out presented the lands west of the Luo, requesting removal of the paoge punishment. Zhou then granted it, gave bow, arrows, axe, and halberd, allowing him to campaign, making him Western Earl. Yet he employed Fei Zhong to govern. Fei Zhong was skilled at flattery, loved profit—Yin people did not cleave to him. Zhou again employed E Lai. E Lai was skilled at slander; by this the lords grew more distant.
translations.idiomatic: The people muttered and lords rebelled; Zhou answered with crueler laws and the paoge torture. He named Western Earl Chang, the Nine Marquises, and the Marquis of E as the three high dukes. The Nine Marquises offered a beautiful daughter to the court. She disliked debauchery; Zhou killed her in fury and had the Nine Marquises ground to paste. The Marquis of E remonstrated fiercely; Zhou had him pickled as well. When Western Earl Chang heard this he sighed in private. Chonghou Hu informed on him, and Zhou jailed the Western Earl at Youli. His men Hongyao and the rest ransomed him with beauties, treasures, and horses, and Zhou set him free. On his release he ceded the lands west of the Luo to buy the abolition of paoge. Zhou agreed and gave him bow, axe, and halberd with authority to wage war as Western Earl. Yet he put Fei Zhong in charge of government. Fei Zhong flattered and grasped after gain; the Yin people did not trust him. Zhou also employed E Lai. E Lai excelled at malice; the lords drew farther away.

sentences.zh: 西伯歸，乃陰修德行善，諸侯多叛紂而往歸西伯。

translations.literal: Western Earl returning secretly cultivated virtue doing good; many lords rebelled against Zhou and went to join Western Earl.
translations.idiomatic: The Western Earl went home and quietly built virtue; many lords forsook Zhou for him.

sentences.zh: 西伯滋大，紂由是稍失權重。

translations.literal: Western Earl grew greater; Zhou thereby gradually lost weight of power.
translations.idiomatic: As the Western Earl grew mighty, Zhou's grip slackened.

sentences.zh: 王子比干諫，弗聽。

translations.literal: Prince Bigan remonstrated; he would not listen.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Bigan remonstrated; Zhou would not hear him.

sentences.zh: 商容賢者，百姓愛之，紂廢之。

translations.literal: Shang Rong was a worthy; the hundred surnames loved him; Zhou cast him aside.
translations.idiomatic: Shang Rong was worthy and beloved; Zhou dismissed him.

sentences.zh: 及西伯伐饑國，滅之，紂之臣祖伊聞之而咎周，恐，奔告紂曰：「天既訖我殷命，假人元龜，無敢知吉，非先王不相我後人，維王淫虐用自絕，故天棄我，不有安食，不虞知天性，不迪率典。

translations.literal: When Western Earl attacked the Ji state and destroyed it, Zhou's minister Zuyi heard it and blamed Zhou, feared, ran to report to Zhou, saying, "Heaven has already ended our Yin mandate; borrowing men's great tortoise, none dares know auspicious—not former kings not aiding our posterity, only the king dissolute and cruel using self to cut off—therefore Heaven abandons us, we lack secure food, we do not think to know Heaven's nature, we do not follow lead and models.
