translations.idiomatic: When the Western Earl destroyed Ji, Zuyi blamed Zhou, fled to court, and said: Heaven has ended Yin's mandate; even the great tortoise cannot show good fortune—it is not that the former kings forsake us, but the king's cruelty has cut us off from Heaven, so we lack peace, ignore Heaven's mind, and spurn the statutes.

sentences.zh: 今我民罔不欲喪，曰『天曷不降威，大命胡不至』？

translations.literal: Now our people none do not desire perdition, saying 'When will Heaven send down its might—why does the great mandate not arrive?'
translations.idiomatic: Now none of your people but wishes this house to fall, crying: When will Heaven strike—why does the new king not come?

sentences.zh: 今王其柰何？」

translations.literal: Now what shall the king do?"
translations.idiomatic: What will you do now, my king?

sentences.zh: 紂曰：「我生不有命在天乎！」

translations.literal: Zhou said, "Was I not born with the mandate in Heaven!"
translations.idiomatic: Zhou answered: Was I not born with Heaven's mandate?

sentences.zh: 祖伊反，曰：「紂不可諫矣。」

translations.literal: Zuyi returning said, "Zhou cannot be remonstrated with."
translations.idiomatic: Zuyi went home saying: Zhou will not be warned.

sentences.zh: 西伯既卒，周武王之東伐，至盟津，諸侯叛殷會周者八百。

translations.literal: Western Earl having passed away, King Wu of Zhou campaigned east, reaching Mengjin; lords rebelling against Yin who met Zhou were eight hundred.
translations.idiomatic: After the Western Earl died, King Wu marched east to Mengjin; eight hundred lords who had turned from Yin rallied to him.

sentences.zh: 諸侯皆曰：「紂可伐矣。」

translations.literal: The lords all said, "Zhou can be attacked."
translations.idiomatic: They said: Zhou is ripe for attack.

sentences.zh: 武王曰：「爾未知天命。」

translations.literal: Martial King said, "You do not yet know Heaven's mandate."
translations.idiomatic: The Martial King said: You do not yet read Heaven's timing.

sentences.zh: 乃復歸。

translations.literal: Thereupon he returned.
translations.idiomatic: So he marched home.

translations.literal: Western Earl returning secretly cultivated virtue doing good; many lords rebelled against Zhou and went to join Western Earl. Western Earl grew greater; Zhou thereby gradually lost weight of power. Prince Bigan remonstrated; he would not listen. Shang Rong was a worthy; the hundred surnames loved him; Zhou cast him aside. When Western Earl attacked the Ji state and destroyed it, Zhou's minister Zuyi heard it and blamed Zhou, feared, ran to report to Zhou, saying, "Heaven has already ended our Yin mandate; borrowing men's great tortoise, none dares know auspicious—not former kings not aiding our posterity, only the king dissolute and cruel using self to cut off—therefore Heaven abandons us, we lack secure food, we do not think to know Heaven's nature, we do not follow lead and models. Now our people none do not desire perdition, saying 'When will Heaven send down its might—why does the great mandate not arrive?' Now what shall the king do?" Zhou said, "Was I not born with the mandate in Heaven!" Zuyi returning said, "Zhou cannot be remonstrated with." Western Earl having passed away, King Wu of Zhou campaigned east, reaching Mengjin; lords rebelling against Yin who met Zhou were eight hundred. The lords all said, "Zhou can be attacked." Martial King said, "You do not yet know Heaven's mandate." Thereupon he returned.
translations.idiomatic: The Western Earl went home and quietly built virtue; many lords forsook Zhou for him. As the Western Earl grew mighty, Zhou's grip slackened. Prince Bigan remonstrated; Zhou would not hear him. Shang Rong was worthy and beloved; Zhou dismissed him. When the Western Earl destroyed Ji, Zuyi blamed Zhou, fled to court, and said: Heaven has ended Yin's mandate; even the great tortoise cannot show good fortune—it is not that the former kings forsake us, but the king's cruelty has cut us off from Heaven, so we lack peace, ignore Heaven's mind, and spurn the statutes. Now none of your people but wishes this house to fall, crying: When will Heaven strike—why does the new king not come? What will you do now, my king? Zhou answered: Was I not born with Heaven's mandate? Zuyi went home saying: Zhou will not be warned. After the Western Earl died, King Wu marched east to Mengjin; eight hundred lords who had turned from Yin rallied to him. They said: Zhou is ripe for attack. The Martial King said: You do not yet read Heaven's timing. So he marched home.

sentences.zh: 紂愈淫亂不止。

translations.literal: Zhou further dissolute and chaotic without stop.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou sank deeper into vice without end.

sentences.zh: 微子數諫不聽，乃與大師、少師謀，遂去。

translations.literal: Weizi remonstrated many times without being heard; then with the grand and junior masters he planned and departed.
translations.idiomatic: Weizi pleaded again and again in vain, then conspired with the grand and junior masters and left.

sentences.zh: 比干曰：「為人臣者，不得不以死爭。」

translations.literal: Bigan said, "Being someone's minister, one cannot but contend with death."
translations.idiomatic: Bigan said: A minister who will not risk death is no minister.

sentences.zh: 乃彊諫紂。

translations.literal: Thereupon he strongly remonstrated Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: He then rebuked Zhou bluntly.

sentences.zh: 紂怒曰：「吾聞聖人心有七竅。」

translations.literal: Zhou angrily said, "I have heard the sage's heart has seven openings."
translations.idiomatic: Zhou raged: I hear a sage's heart has seven chambers.

sentences.zh: 剖比干，觀其心。

translations.literal: He cut open Bigan and viewed his heart.
translations.idiomatic: He cut Bigan open to look at his heart.

sentences.zh: 箕子懼，乃詳狂為奴，紂又囚之。

translations.literal: Jizi feared, then feigned madness as a slave; Zhou again imprisoned him.
translations.idiomatic: Jizi feigned madness as a slave in terror; Zhou jailed him too.

sentences.zh: 殷之大師、少師乃持其祭樂器奔周。

translations.literal: Yin's grand and junior masters then took their sacrificial instruments and fled to Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: Yin's grand and junior masters fled to Zhou carrying the ritual vessels.

sentences.zh: 周武王於是遂率諸侯伐紂。

translations.literal: King Wu of Zhou thereupon then led the lords to attack Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: King Wu then led the host against Zhou.

sentences.zh: 紂亦發兵距之牧野。

translations.literal: Zhou also raised troops to resist him at Muye.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou sent an army to meet him at Muye.

sentences.zh: 甲子日，紂兵敗。

translations.literal: On the jiazi day Zhou's army was defeated.
translations.idiomatic: On the jiazi day Zhou's host was broken.

sentences.zh: 紂走入，登鹿臺，衣其寶玉衣，赴火而死。

translations.literal: Zhou ran in, ascended Lutai, clothed himself in his jade-inlaid robes, threw himself into fire and died.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou fled within, climbed Lutai in his jeweled robes, and burned himself to death.

sentences.zh: 周武王遂斬紂頭，縣之［大］白旗。

translations.literal: King Wu of Zhou thereupon beheaded Zhou, hung it on the great white banner.
translations.idiomatic: King Wu struck off his head and raised it on the great white standard.

sentences.zh: 殺妲己。

translations.literal: He killed Daji.
translations.idiomatic: He executed Daji.

sentences.zh: 釋箕子之囚，封比干之墓，表商容之閭。

translations.literal: He released Jizi from prison, sealed Bigan's tomb, marked Shang Rong's lane.
translations.idiomatic: He freed Jizi, sealed Bigan's grave, and honored Shang Rong's lane.

sentences.zh: 封紂子武庚、祿父，以續殷祀，令修行盤庚之政。

translations.literal: He enfeoffed Zhou's son Wugeng, Lufu, to continue Yin sacrifices, ordering him to practice Pangeng's government.
translations.idiomatic: He enfeoffed Zhou's son Wugeng—Lufu—to keep Yin sacrifices and follow Pangeng's rule.

sentences.zh: 殷民大說。

translations.literal: Yin people greatly rejoiced.
translations.idiomatic: The Yin people rejoiced.

sentences.zh: 於是周武王為天子。

translations.literal: Thereupon King Wu of Zhou became Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: King Wu then became Son of Heaven.

sentences.zh: 其後世貶帝號，號為王。

translations.literal: His later generations lowered the title emperor, styling them kings.
translations.idiomatic: Later Zhou dropped the title emperor and called its rulers kings.

sentences.zh: 而封殷後為諸侯，屬周。

translations.literal: And he enfeoffed Yin's posterity as lords, subordinate to Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: Yin's descendants were enfeoffed as lords under Zhou.

translations.literal: Zhou further dissolute and chaotic without stop. Weizi remonstrated many times without being heard; then with the grand and junior masters he planned and departed. Bigan said, "Being someone's minister, one cannot but contend with death." Thereupon he strongly remonstrated Zhou. Zhou angrily said, "I have heard the sage's heart has seven openings." He cut open Bigan and viewed his heart. Jizi feared, then feigned madness as a slave; Zhou again imprisoned him. Yin's grand and junior masters then took their sacrificial instruments and fled to Zhou. King Wu of Zhou thereupon then led the lords to attack Zhou. Zhou also raised troops to resist him at Muye. On the jiazi day Zhou's army was defeated. Zhou ran in, ascended Lutai, clothed himself in his jade-inlaid robes, threw himself into fire and died. King Wu of Zhou thereupon beheaded Zhou, hung it on the great white banner. He killed Daji. He released Jizi from prison, sealed Bigan's tomb, marked Shang Rong's lane. He enfeoffed Zhou's son Wugeng, Lufu, to continue Yin sacrifices, ordering him to practice Pangeng's government. Yin people greatly rejoiced. Thereupon King Wu of Zhou became Son of Heaven. His later generations lowered the title emperor, styling them kings. And he enfeoffed Yin's posterity as lords, subordinate to Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou sank deeper into vice without end. Weizi pleaded again and again in vain, then conspired with the grand and junior masters and left. Bigan said: A minister who will not risk death is no minister. He then rebuked Zhou bluntly. Zhou raged: I hear a sage's heart has seven chambers. He cut Bigan open to look at his heart. Jizi feigned madness as a slave in terror; Zhou jailed him too. Yin's grand and junior masters fled to Zhou carrying the ritual vessels. King Wu then led the host against Zhou. Zhou sent an army to meet him at Muye. On the jiazi day Zhou's host was broken. Zhou fled within, climbed Lutai in his jeweled robes, and burned himself to death. King Wu struck off his head and raised it on the great white standard. He executed Daji. He freed Jizi, sealed Bigan's grave, and honored Shang Rong's lane. He enfeoffed Zhou's son Wugeng—Lufu—to keep Yin sacrifices and follow Pangeng's rule. The Yin people rejoiced. King Wu then became Son of Heaven. Later Zhou dropped the title emperor and called its rulers kings. Yin's descendants were enfeoffed as lords under Zhou.

sentences.zh: 周武王崩，武庚與管叔、蔡叔作亂，成王命周公誅之，而立微子於宋，以續殷後焉。

translations.literal: King Wu of Zhou passed away; Wugeng with the lords of Guan and Cai made rebellion; the completed king ordered the Duke of Zhou to execute them, then established Weizi at Song to continue Yin's posterity.
translations.idiomatic: When King Wu died, Wugeng and the lords of Guan and Cai rebelled; the young king had the Duke of Zhou crush them and set Weizi in Song to carry on Yin's line.

translations.literal: King Wu of Zhou passed away; Wugeng with the lords of Guan and Cai made rebellion; the completed king ordered the Duke of Zhou to execute them, then established Weizi at Song to continue Yin's posterity.
translations.idiomatic: When King Wu died, Wugeng and the lords of Guan and Cai rebelled; the young king had the Duke of Zhou crush them and set Weizi in Song to carry on Yin's line.

sentences.zh: 太史公曰：余以頌次契之事，自成湯以來，采於書詩。

translations.literal: The Grand Scribe says: I have arranged Xie's affairs by the hymns; from Cheng Tang onward drawn from the Documents and Odes.
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Scribe remarks: I ordered Xie's story by the hymns; from Cheng Tang I drew on the Documents and the Odes.

sentences.zh: 契為子姓，其後分封，以國為姓，有殷氏、來氏、宋氏、空桐氏、稚氏、北殷氏、目夷氏。

translations.literal: Xie was Zi surname; afterward divided enfeoffments, used states as surnames—there were Yin, Lai, Song, Kongtong, Zhi, Beiyin, Moyi clans.
translations.idiomatic: Xie's house took the name Zi; later branches used their domains as surnames, yielding houses such as Yin, Lai, Song, Kongtong, Zhi, Beiyin, and Moyi.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰，殷路車為善，而色尚白。

translations.literal: Confucius said, Yin's road carriages were finest, and the color honored was white.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said the Yin state carriages were the best of their day and they favored the color white.

translations.literal: The Grand Scribe says: I have arranged Xie's affairs by the hymns; from Cheng Tang onward drawn from the Documents and Odes. Xie was Zi surname; afterward divided enfeoffments, used states as surnames—there were Yin, Lai, Song, Kongtong, Zhi, Beiyin, Moyi clans. Confucius said, Yin's road carriages were finest, and the color honored was white.
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Scribe remarks: I ordered Xie's story by the hymns; from Cheng Tang I drew on the Documents and the Odes. Xie's house took the name Zi; later branches used their domains as surnames, yielding houses such as Yin, Lai, Song, Kongtong, Zhi, Beiyin, and Moyi. Confucius said the Yin state carriages were the best of their day and they favored the color white.
